content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcal PnecrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56ffi o Fax (916) 322-0886
AprJl 13,2004
Jim R. Karpiak, Asst. City Attorney
City of Fairfield
Richards, Watson & Gershon
44Montgomery Skeet, Suite 1420
San Francisco, CA 94104-4701
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-04-077
Dear Mr. Karpiak:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Stanley Silverman
for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1
QUESTIONS
1. Is Mr. Silverman prohibited from participating in consideration of the
"Villages at Fairfield" project (the "project') because the residence he owns and resides
in is 200 feet from the project site?
2. May Mr. Silverman attend the planning commission's meetings or meetings of
other bodies concerning the project, or informational meetings about and tours of the-
project site?
3. May Mr. Silverman speak as a member of the public on the project or any
component thereof?
CONCLUSIONS
1. It is presumed that the financial effect of the project decisions on the
commissioner's property is material. Therefore, absent an exception, he is presumed to
have a conflict of interest and may not participate in this decision.
I Government Code $$ 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, $$ 18109-
18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
!g_l


FileNo. A-04-077
Page No. 2
2. If the commissioner has a conflict of interest in a decision to be considered at a
noticed public meeting, then the commissioner must: (l) immediatelyprior to the
discussion of the itern, publicly identifu each tlpe of economic interest involved in the
decision as well as details of the economic interest as discussed in regulation
18702.5@)(1XB) on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself; and (3) leave the room
for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (See reg. l8702,5,enclosed.)
Assuming all the gatherings you refer to will be noticed meetings and events, and that
neither ofthe exceptions apply, the commissioner will not be able to stay and observe the
meetings.
3. A public official is not attonpting to use his or her official position to make a
govemmental decision if the official appears in the same manner as any other member of
the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function
solely to represent himself or herself on a matter which is related to his or her personal
interests. The commissioner's "personal interests" include his wholly owned interest in
real property. However, if this exception applies, the official must be careful to avoid
gving the impression that he is speaking in the interest of any other individual or group,
or that he is acting in any official capacity.
FACTS
The Fairfield Planning Commission will shortly consider an application from a
developer to construct the "Villages at Fairfi eld," aphased proJect, which would consist
of 2,341housing units and a I I1,000 square foot neighborhood shopping cente,r, on 435
acres of land. With respect to theproject, theplanning commission will consider a
general plan change, azoringchange, certification of an environmental impact rqrort,
tentative subdivision map, development agreement, and design review. Mr. Silverrnan
owns and resides in a personal residence that is approximately 200 feet from the
boundary ofthe project site.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit a public official from making
participating in making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a
govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. ($ 87100.)
A public official has a "financial interest" in a goveflrmental decision, within the
meaning ofthe Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests.
($ 87103; reg. 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualiffing conflict of interest in a given governmental
decision. (Reg. 18700(bxl)-(8).) The steps of this analysis are outlined below.


File No. A-04-077
Page No. 3
1. Is Mr. Silverman a "Public Official'?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." ($$
87100 and 87103; reg. 187000)(1).) "Public official" is defined as "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency. . . ." ($ 82048.) As
a Fairfield Planning Commissioner, Mr. Silverman is a "public official" and therefore
subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
2. Is Commissioner Silverman Making Participating in Making or Influencing a
Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a
govemmental decision" rvhen, acting within the authority of his or her position and
without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Reg.
18702.2.) A public ofiicial is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision before his or her own agency i{ for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts or appears before any mernber, officetr, employeg or consultant of his or
her agency. (Reg. 187A23.)
Attendance: If the commissioner has a conflict of interest in a decision to be
considered at a noticed public meeting, then the commissioner must: (1) immediately
prior to the discussion of the item, publicly identiff on the record of the meeting each
tlpe of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic
interest as discussed in regulation 18702.5(bXlXB); (2) recuse himsel{ and (3) leave the
room for the duration of the discussion xtd/or vote on the item. Regulation 1S702.5(dXl)
and (3) provide the following exceptions:
Uncontested Matters: Section 87105(a)(3) provides an exce,ption for uncontested
matters on consent calendars. When the matter in which the public official has a
financial interest is on the consent calendar, thepublic official must comply with
subdivisions (b)(1) (disclosure) and (bX2) (recusal) of this regulation, but the public
official is not required to leave the room during the consent calendar.
a
Speaking as a Member of the Public Regarding an Applicable Personal Interest:
When a personal interest as identified in regulation187O2.4(b) is affected, apublic
official may speak as a member of the general public so long as he complies with
subdivisions OXI) (disclosure) and (b)(2) (recusal), and he leaves the dais to speak
from the same area as the members of the public. The official may listen to the public
discussion of the matter with the members of the public. This exception is discussed
further below.


File No. A-04-077
Page No. 4
Assuming all the gatherings you refer to will be noticed meetings and events, and
that neither of the exceptions agply, the commissioner will not be able to stay and
observe.
Representing the Official's Own Interests: Even if a conflict of interest is
present, a public official may appear before his or her agency as a member of the general
public in the course of its prescribed governmental function in order to represent himself
or herself on matters related solely to his or her personal interests. Under the exception
provided at regulations 18702.4(a)(2) and 18702.4OX1), such an appearance does not
constifute making, participating in making or influencing a governmental decision.
Regulation 18702.4(b)(l) defines an official's "personal interests" to include, but not be
limited to:
"(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate family.
"(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members of
his or her immediate family.
"(C) A business entity overwhich the official exercises sole
direction and control, or over which the official and his or her spouse
jointly exercise sole direction and control."
If this exception applies, the commissionermust be careful to avoid gving the impression
that he is speaking in the interest of any otherperson or group, or that he is acting in any
official capaeity.
3. What are Commissioner Silverman,s Economic Interests?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
$ 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
ef;[ect, distinguishable from its effect on thepublic generally, on the official or on any
one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (g 871 03(a); reg. I 8703. I (a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employeg or holds any position
ofmanagement ($ 87103(d); reg. 18703.IO));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more ($ 87103O); reg. 187A3.2);
o
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision ($ 87103(c); reg. 18703.3);


a
o
File No. A-04-077
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(e);
reg.18703.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or herpersonal finances, including
those of his orher immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
($ 87103; reg. 18703.5).
Assuming theplanning commissionerhas an interest of $2,000 ormore in his
residence, he will have an economic interest in this real property. You have not provided
information regarding any other economic interests. For purposes of this letter, we
assume the commissioner has no other economic interest relevant to the decisions you
have identified.
4. Are Commissioner Silverman's Economic fnterests Directly or Indirectly
Involved in the Govermmental Decision?
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if, among other situations:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision." (Reg. 187 Aa.2@)(l).\
You have stated that the commissioner's residence is located within 500 feet of
the project site. Thus, the commissioner's economic interest is directly involved in the
decision.
5. What is the Applicable Materiality Standard?
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard of regulation 18705.2(a)
applies. (Reg. l87$a.2@)(1).) Regulation 1,87A5.2(a)(1) provides that the financial ef[ect
of a governmental decision on real property (other than a leasehold) that is directly
involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. '"fhis presumption
may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have any financial effect on the real property.,,
6. Is it Reasonably Foreseeable That the Financial Effect of the Governmental
Decision Upon Any of Commissioner Silverman's Economic Interests \ililt Meet the
Applicable Materiality Standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Reg. 18706(a).) A financial effect need not


File No. A-04-077
Page No. 6
be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
It is presumed that the financial effect of the decision on the commissioner's
property is material. In order to rebut the presumption of regulation 18705.2(a)(l), the
commissioner is required to show that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have any frnancial effect on his property. Thus, the commissioner would have to
show that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the project located within 500
feet of his property will have everr a penny's effect on his real property.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the detennination of whether ornot it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the
commissione,r's real property is necessarily a facfual question that is ultimately for each
commissioner to decide. However, we reiterate, the commissioner's real property is
presumed to experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision.
7. & 8. *Public GenerallyD and 66legally Required Participation, Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies when the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. ($ 831 12; reg. 18707.) Step eight is an exception that
applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is legally required.
($ 87101; reg. 18708.) Both of these narrowly constnred exceptions are fact-driven, and
you provide no facts addressing these exceptions. Accordingly, we offer no advice
whether these exceptions apply with respect to the decisions you describe.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
Jwwjg
